1:
Ps 29 and the Ugaritic Baal Cycle. This psalm is widely recognized as the most deeply ANE-influenced in the Psalter. Its closest parallels are in Ugaritic Baal epic texts from Ras Shamra (14th-13th c. BC), which celebrate Baal-Hadad's storm victory over the sea god Yam. The sevenfold repetition of 'the voice of the LORD' (qol YHWH) echoes Baal's 'voice' (Baal's thunder = his war-shout). The psalm functions polemically: YHWH controls what Baal claimed.

'O heavenly beings' (bene elim) — literally 'sons of gods/divine beings,' the divine council of the ANE cosmos. In Ugaritic texts, El's council (the sons of El) are the assembled divine beings. Ps 82 depicts YHWH judging this divine council. The call for the divine beings to glorify YHWH asserts his supremacy within the cosmic court.
2: 'Worship the LORD in the splendor of his holiness' — hadar qodsho (the splendor/glory of his holiness). ANE gods were worshiped with elaborate cultic splendor: garments, processions, music, and material glory. The psalm calls the divine beings to worship YHWH with the full display of divine honor that ANE court theology expected of vassal-deities before the great king. Hodot le-shem (give to the name) — the divine name in the ANE was not merely a label but the seat of the deity's identity and power: to glorify the name is to acknowledge the deity's full power.
3: 'The voice of the LORD is over the waters' — the sevenfold qol YHWH (vv3-9) is the theological heart of the psalm. In the Baal Cycle, Baal's voice is his thunder, his weapon in cosmic battle. Ps 29 appropriates this storm-theology and attributes it entirely to YHWH. 'Over the waters' — the cosmic waters (mayim rabbim, mighty waters) were a primary symbol of chaos in both Canaanite and Mesopotamian cosmology. YHWH's voice commanding the waters recalls Gen 1 and the Exodus sea-splitting. 'The God of glory' (el ha-kavod) — the only occurrence of this title in the Psalter.
4: 'The voice of the LORD is powerful; the voice of the LORD is majestic' — the repetition of qol YHWH without an accompanying action creates a sonic crescendo. Ugaritic poetry uses the same anaphoric repetition for Baal's storm. In Hebrew poetry, sevenfold repetition is associated with completeness: the seven-day creation, the sevenfold sprinkling of blood in priestly ritual. Seven utterances of qol YHWH implies YHWH's voice pervades all seven zones of the cosmos.
5: 'The voice of the LORD breaks the cedars of Lebanon' — Lebanon's cedars were the most famous and sacred trees in the ANE: the Baal Cycle describes Baal's palace built from Lebanese cedar; Mesopotamian epics feature the Cedar Forest as the dwelling of gods and monsters. To break the cedars of Lebanon is to demonstrate power over the most sacred natural symbol of the Canaanite divine world. Lebanon was Baal's territory, and YHWH's voice shatters it.
6: 'He makes Lebanon skip like a calf, and Sirion like a young wild ox' — Sirion is the Phoenician/Sidonian name for Mount Hermon (Deuteronomy 3:9), the sacred peak of the Canaanite divine assembly. Hermon/Sirion was associated with the oath sworn by the Watchers in 1 Enoch 6, suggesting its long-standing significance as a supernatural site. YHWH's storm-voice makes these sacred mountains dance — the divine council's residence trembles before him. The calf/wild ox imagery may also echo the bull iconography of Baal and El, which Israel controversially adopted (the golden calves of Exod 32; 1 Kings 12:28).
7: 'The voice of the LORD strikes with flashes of lightning' — lightning as the divine weapon is pervasive in ANE storm-god theology: Baal wields the lightning bolt, as does the Mesopotamian storm-god Adad/Hadad. Zeus in Greek tradition carries the thunderbolt. Ps 29 does not deny the storm-phenomena but asserts that the one wielding the lightning is YHWH, not Baal. The Exodus tradition also uses storm-theophany (Exod 19 — the Sinai thunderstorm) to establish YHWH's identity as the true cosmic storm-lord.
8: 'The voice of the LORD shakes the wilderness; the LORD shakes the wilderness of Kadesh' — Kadesh-Barnea is the desert wilderness at the southern border of Canaan, the site of Israel's long encampment during the wilderness wandering. The geography moves from north (Lebanon, Hermon) to south (Kadesh wilderness): YHWH's storm-voice covers the entire land. The wilderness was both the zone of divine encounter (the Sinai revelation) and the zone of divine testing; its shaking signals YHWH's sovereign reach over the entire Exodus route.
9: 'The voice of the LORD makes the deer give birth and strips the forests bare' — the ayalot (deer/does) image: the pain of labor induced by divine storm. ANE birth texts associate violent natural events with the timing of labor. 'In his temple all cry Glory!' — the heavenly court acclaims YHWH's storm-victory: the divine council responds to the theophany with a shout of kavod. This mirrors the pattern of Isa 6 where the seraphim call 'holy, holy, holy' as YHWH appears in the temple.
10: 'The LORD sits enthroned over the flood; the LORD sits enthroned as King forever' — mabul (flood) is the rare technical term for Noah's flood (the only other uses are Gen 6-11). By using the flood-term, the psalm places YHWH's eternal kingship in the context of his cosmic victory over the waters of chaos: as he subdued the waters of the flood, so he reigns over all subsequent storms. The Babylonian Creation Epic (Enuma Elish) similarly celebrates Marduk's kingship following his victory over the sea-goddess Tiamat.
11: 'The LORD gives strength to his people; the LORD blesses his people with peace' — the storm theophany closes not with destruction but with shalom: the cosmic storm that threatens is channeled into blessing for the covenant community. This is the psalm's pastoral turn: the terrifying power celebrated in vv3-10 becomes protective strength and peace for Israel. YHWH's storm-power is not random cosmic violence (Baal's storms) but directed covenant protection.
